sources.notes: Find the minimum complete set B for an array A under divisor and multiple closure rules.
Official editorial states that the smallest unfinished element must be chosen, then all its multiples up to k must appear in A.
Independent writeup explains processing unique A in ascending order and marking multiples already covered.

annotation.summary: Find the smallest complete divisor set B, or report impossibility.
annotation.constraints: Values are bounded by k, so checking multiples of chosen base elements is feasible with marking/skipping.
annotation.core_idea: After deduplicating A, scan values ascending. If a value is already a multiple of a previously chosen b, it is covered. Otherwise it must be included in B, because no smaller divisor in A can cover it; then every positive multiple of this value up to k must occur in A, or no complete set exists. Mark all such multiples as covered.
annotation.complexity: Roughly proportional to the number of checked multiples/divisor incidences, fast enough under the constraints.

solution_variants.name: ascending-forced-bases
solution_variants.summary: Process values in increasing order, greedily choose each uncovered value and verify all multiples exist.
solution_variants.complexity: O(sum over chosen b of k/b) with set lookups

tags.evidence: Both rules are about divisors and multiples, and the proof uses the smallest uncovered divisor structure.
The smallest uncovered value is forced into B, so the ascending greedy choice is optimal.
The implementation verifies and marks all multiples of each chosen base.
tags.description: Iterate over multiples of selected values to mark coverage, count constraints, or propagate number-theory states.
tags.created_reason: Multiple-marking appears often in divisor-closure and sieve-like constructive problems.
